section: Enabling superior impact toughness of low-density δ-ferrite steel by dispersing ultra-fine spheroidized carbides in the ferrite/carbide composite lamellae

context_update.summary: This chunk comprises the title, abstract, and introduction of the paper. It introduces low-density δ-ferrite steel with high Al content (>4 wt%), which leads to coarse δ-ferrite and low toughness. The study investigates the effect of rolling temperature on microstructure and toughness. High-temperature rolling (850–950°C) produces a lamellar structure with honeycomb martensite and DIFT equiaxed ferrite, resulting in impact energy <7 J and elongation <9%, attributed to DIF grains acting as pre-crack paths and hardness mismatch. Low-temperature rolling (650–750°C) yields alternating δ-ferrite and ferrite/carbide composite lamellae with ultra-fine spherical carbides (<250 nm). This enables Orowan strengthening, crack blunting, deflection, and microvoid-induced horizontal crack propagation, achieving impact energy up to 207.5 J and elongation 19.8%. The introduction reviews prior lamellar structure designs and states the innovation: combining spherical carbide strengthening with lamellar structure at lower rolling temperatures. The paper aims to establish processing-structure-property relationships and toughening mechanisms. No figures or tables are discussed yet.
microstructure: alternating δ-ferrite/ferrite/carbide composite lamellae with spherical carbides <250 nm (low-T)
coarse δ-ferrite >50 μm at room temperature (as-cast)
properties: impact energy up to 207.5 J, elongation 19.8% (low-T rolled)
mechanisms: DIF grains as pre-existing crack paths and interfacial stress concentration lead to brittle fracture (high-T)
Orowan mechanism impedes crack propagation through carbide-rich lamellae
crack tip blunting by carbides increases curvature radius
crack deflection into horizontal direction
microvoid nucleation at carbides promotes horizontal crack propagation along boundaries
synergistic dislocation storage and second-phase strengthening via θ-curve analysis

Material systems: low-density δ-ferrite steel; high-aluminum (>4 wt%) steel; δ-ferrite; martensite; ultra-fine spherical carbides (cementite); deformation-induced ferrite (DIF)
Processing route: high-temperature rolling (850–950°C); low-temperature rolling (650–750°C); multi-pass rolling (implied)
Key variables: rolling temperature; impact energy; elongation; phase hardness mismatch; carbide diameter; Orowan mechanism
Microstructure: lamellar δ-ferrite/honeycomb martensite/DIF grains (high-T); alternating δ-ferrite/ferrite/carbide composite lamellae with spherical carbides <250 nm (low-T); coarse δ-ferrite >50 μm at room temperature (as-cast)
Properties: impact energy <7 J, elongation <9% (high-T rolled); impact energy up to 207.5 J, elongation 19.8% (low-T rolled); martensite/ferrite hardness difference >3.3 GPa
Mechanisms: DIF grains as pre-existing crack paths and interfacial stress concentration lead to brittle fracture (high-T); Orowan mechanism impedes crack propagation through carbide-rich lamellae; crack tip blunting by carbides increases curvature radius; crack deflection into horizontal direction; microvoid nucleation at carbides promotes horizontal crack propagation along boundaries; synergistic dislocation storage and second-phase strengthening via θ-curve analysis

context_update.summary: The current chunk (ending of Introduction, Experimental methods, and initial Results section 3.1) presents the innovation of combining spherical carbide strengthening with lamellar structure via low-temperature rolling, and multi-stage toughening (Orowan, crack blunting/deflection, microvoid-induced propagation) enabling impact energy >200 J. It provides the steel composition (Fe-0.35C-1.47Mn-4.50Al-0.26Cr-0.28Mo-1.47Ni wt%), full processing route (VIM, 1200°C/4h, hot forging 40mm, reheating 650-950°C/2h, 60% hot rolling to 16mm) producing samples R650, R750, R850, R950. Tensile and Charpy specimen geometry and characterization parameters (SEM/EBSD, nanoindentation, TEM) are given. Microstructural results show low-temperature rolled samples have alternating δ-ferrite/carbide-ferrite composite lamellae with ultra-fine spherical carbides (R650: 158nm, 1.7/μm², C/F 51.5%; R750: 247nm, 0.6/μm², C/F 43.3%), while high-temperature rolled samples possess δ-ferrite/honeycomb martensite lamellae with DIF and no carbides (R850 M 33.2%, R950 M 59.0%). EBSD reveals HAGBs, KAM, dislocation densities, GOS, texture evolution, and nanoindentation maps phase hardness. Key hardness mismatches (0.72 GPa low-T, 3.86 GPa high-T) drive Orowan strengthening and stress concentration. Subsequent chunks should link these microstructures to tensile/impact properties and detailed fracture mechanisms, referencing Tables 1-3 and Figures 1-4.
microstructure: ultra-fine spherical carbides coarsening at grain boundaries (Ostwald ripening)
properties: nano-hardness: C/F 3.64 GPa, δ-F 2.92-2.93 GPa (low-T); M 5.98-6.50 GPa, DIF 2.84-2.98 GPa, δ-F 2.64-2.77 GPa (high-T)
mechanisms: Orowan strengthening by carbides in composite lamellae
crack tip blunting, deflection, and microvoid-induced horizontal crack propagation
DIF equiaxed grains as prefabricated crack paths
stress concentration at phase boundaries due to hardness mismatch

context_update.summary: This chunk provides the quantitative EBSD and nanoindentation data (HAGBs, GOS≤2°, dislocation density, phase nanohardness) for specimens R650 to R950 and presents the full set of room-temperature mechanical properties. R650 demonstrates outstanding impact toughness with an IAE of 207.5 J, distinguished into initiation (59.6 J) and propagation (147.9 J) energies, along with 19.0% elongation and an SPP of 16.51 GPa·%. R750 retains 17.3% elongation but impact energy falls to 75.9 J and propagation energy to 51.5 J. R850 and R950 are brittle (impact <6 J, elongation 8.3% and 3.5%) with tensile strength rising to 1254.5 MPa but SPP declining to 4.4 GPa·%. Work hardening curves reveal a hump for low-T rolled steels attributed to Orowan pinning by <250 nm carbides, while high-T rolled steels show monotonic decrease. The discussion explains lamellar structure formation via strain partitioning, identifies carbides M3C, M23C6, M7C3 by TEM/EDS, describes Ostwald ripening leading to coarse grain-boundary carbides at 750°C, and clarifies the transition to honeycomb martensite with DIFT and dynamic recrystallization at high temperature. Subsequent sections should connect these microstructural features and properties to detailed fracture mechanisms and crack propagation paths.
microstructure: alternating δ-ferrite/carbide-ferrite composite lamellae with ultra-fine spherical carbides (R650, R750)
δ-ferrite/honeycomb martensite lamellae with DIF (R850, R950)
coarse carbides at grain boundaries + fine carbides inside grains (Ostwald ripening at 750°C)
submicron equiaxed DIF grains surrounded by martensite
properties: low-T work hardening hump vs high-T monotonic decrease
mechanisms: Orowan dislocation pinning by <250 nm carbides enhancing strain hardening and delaying necking
crack tip blunting and deflection implied by high Ep
Ostwald ripening coarsening carbides at grain boundaries
lamellar structure formation by strain partitioning between soft ferrite and hard martensite
carbide precipitation from fragmented supersaturated martensite
DIFT producing hard submicron equiaxed ferrite
dynamic recovery dominant at low T, dynamic recrystallization and HAGBs formation at high T

context_update.summary: This chunk reports carbide type identification (M3C, M23C6, M7C3) in R650 by TEM/SAED/EDS (Fig. 7) and extensively discusses the fracture mechanisms in section 4.2. For R650, the microstructure contains numerous submicron spherical carbides (<250 nm) distributed within ferrite lamellae, with a few short rod-like cementite at grain boundaries. During impact, cracks exhibit plane strain in δ-ferrite and plane stress with interface separation in carbide/ferrite lamellae, causing blunting and crack arrest. Spherical carbides increase effective crack-tip curvature radius, nucleate microvoids, and promote horizontal secondary cracking, enhancing toughness. R650 also shows superior UTS, TE, SPP over R750 due to finer carbides and stronger dispersion strengthening. For R750, carbide coarsening and reduction lead to ~60% lower impact energy. R850/R950 exhibit carbide dissolution, forming honeycomb martensite and DIFT grains, resulting in brittle fracture (<10 J) due to stress concentration, DIFT shortcut, and martensite brittleness. TEM confirms Orowan dislocations at carbides >10 nm. Tensile fractures are serrated with delamination. Key figures: Fig. 7, 8, 9. The chapter 4.2 provides mechanistic linkage between rolling temperature, lamellar structure, carbide characteristics, and impact/tensile performance, with emphasis on crack propagation paths and energy dissipation.
microstructure: alternating δ-ferrite/carbide-ferrite lamellae with spherical carbides <250 nm
short rod-like cementite at ferrite grain boundaries (R650)
carbide coarsening and reduced content in R750
honeycomb martensite with equiaxed DIF grains in R850/R950
properties: R750: impact energy ~60% lower than R650
R850/R950: impact energy <10 J, brittle fracture
mechanisms: planar strain/planar stress state transition
interface separation dispersing stress
crack tip blunting by spherical carbides
increase of effective curvature radius reducing stress concentration
microvoid/microcrack nucleation at carbide/ferrite interfaces with horizontal propagation
Orowan pinning of dislocations by carbides >10 nm
stress transfer from ferrite to carbides alleviating interfacial stress concentration
dispersion strengthening by ultrafine carbides
lamellar deformation coordination (hard/soft phase)
DIFT equiaxed grains as crack propagation shortcut by cleavage
honeycomb martensite brittleness inhibiting plastic deformation

Summary: This chunk reports carbide type identification (M3C, M23C6, M7C3) in R650 by TEM/SAED/EDS (Fig. 7) and extensively discusses the fracture mechanisms in section 4.2. For R650, the microstructure contains numerous submicron spherical carbides (<250 nm) distributed within ferrite lamellae, with a few short rod-like cementite at grain boundaries. During impact, cracks exhibit plane strain in δ-ferrite and plane stress with interface separation in carbide/ferrite lamellae, causing blunting and crack arrest. Spherical carbides increase effective crack-tip curvature radius, nucleate microvoids, and promote horizontal secondary cracking, enhancing toughness. R650 also shows superior UTS, TE, SPP over R750 due to finer carbides and stronger dispersion strengthening. For R750, carbide coarsening and reduction lead to ~60% lower impact energy. R850/R950 exhibit carbide dissolution, forming honeycomb martensite and DIFT grains, resulting in brittle fracture (<10 J) due to stress concentration, DIFT shortcut, and martensite brittleness. TEM confirms Orowan dislocations at carbides >10 nm. Tensile fractures are serrated with delamination. Key figures: Fig. 7, 8, 9. The chapter 4.2 provides mechanistic linkage between rolling temperature, lamellar structure, carbide characteristics, and impact/tensile performance, with emphasis on crack propagation paths and energy dissipation.
Material systems: low-density δ-ferrite steel; high-aluminum (>4 wt%) steel; δ-ferrite; martensite; ultra-fine spherical carbides (cementite); deformation-induced ferrite (DIF); carbide (cementite); martensite/austenite constituents
Sample aliases: R650; R750; R850; R950; hot-forged (prior to rolling)
Processing route: high-temperature rolling (850–950°C); low-temperature rolling (650–750°C); multi-pass rolling (implied); vacuum induction melting (VIM); homogenization at 1200°C for 4h; hot forging to 40mm thickness; reheating at 650/750/850/950°C for 2h; hot rolling with 60% reduction to 16mm
Key variables: rolling temperature; impact energy; elongation; phase hardness mismatch; carbide diameter; Orowan mechanism; 60% reduction ratio; C/F volume fraction
Microstructure: lamellar δ-ferrite/honeycomb martensite/DIF grains (high-T); alternating δ-ferrite/ferrite/carbide composite lamellae with spherical carbides <250 nm (low-T); coarse δ-ferrite >50 μm at room temperature (as-cast); lamellar δ-ferrite/carbide-ferrite composite (low-T); honeycomb martensite with equiaxed DIF (high-T); coarse δ-ferrite + M/A (as-forged); ultra-fine spherical carbides coarsening at grain boundaries (Ostwald ripening); texture change from <001> to <101>
Properties: impact energy <7 J, elongation <9% (high-T rolled); impact energy up to 207.5 J, elongation 19.8% (low-T rolled); martensite/ferrite hardness difference >3.3 GPa; nano-hardness: C/F 3.64 GPa, δ-F 2.92-2.93 GPa (low-T); M 5.98-6.50 GPa, DIF 2.84-2.98 GPa, δ-F 2.64-2.77 GPa (high-T); hardness mismatch 0.72 GPa (low-T) and 3.86 GPa (high-T); impact energy >200 J (reported in introduction); R650: IAE 207.5±29.3 J, El 19.0±3.2%, SPP 16.51 GPa·%, Ei 59.6 J, Ep 147.9 J; R750: IAE 75.9±22.3 J, El 17.3±3.6%, Ep 51.51 J

context_update.summary: This chunk contains only the caption for Figure 9 and the heading for Conclusions section. No detailed conclusions or data are provided. The figure caption indicates that TEM, HRTEM, and IFFT images of the impact-fracture deformed zone for R650 will be shown, along with schematic impact-fracture diagrams for all four rolling conditions (R650, R750, R850, R950). This suggests subsequent visual evidence of crack-tip deformation features and a comparative schematic of fracture modes. The actual conclusions are not yet extracted.
